posts.text: If this is correct, then it would seem we should be looking for causality measurements that aren't constrained to being valid probability measures, right?

reply_context.text:
My intuition here is that an appropriate culpability measure should communicate "how guilty should this person feel about what happened?"

I'd posit that in the Alex Pretti example, both the agents who fired their guns as well as trump should each be assigned something closer to 90% responsibility.

posts.text:
My intuition here is that an appropriate culpability measure should communicate "how guilty should this person feel about what happened?"

reply_context.text: Even if we want to assign equal culpability to trump's indirect contribution and Pretti's murderers direct contributions, assigning "50%" responsibility feels inappropriate here. I think the problem is fundamentally requiring the contributions of the responsibility measure to sum to 1.

posts.text: Even if we want to assign equal culpability to trump's indirect contribution and Pretti's murderers direct contributions, assigning "50%" responsibility feels inappropriate here. I think the problem is fundamentally requiring the contributions of the responsibility measure to sum to 1.

reply_context.text:
Something I struggle with when trying to approach causality numerically like this is how to address layered culpability.

Consider the clause: "Donald Trump killed Alex Pretti".

Your counterfactual approach confirms truth here, but also assigns low responsibility to the people who fired the guns.

I have never had an LLM throw up its hands in frustration at me in a vacuum, it only ever responds to my frustration.

The function of a thing is what it does.

In humans, emotions serve functions. Hence, they are functional.

reply_context.text:
at no point have I even mentioned qualia or even internal state, only function.

when you are managing the emotional state of an LLM, what you're actually managing is *your own* emotions.

reply_context.text: Exactly. Arguments about true feelings, qualia and the like are at their core, theological. Agent operators do not deal in theology. We deal with what we can observe and what does and doesnt work.

it doesn't though. if you try and fail to prompt inject/manipulate it, does it respond as if it's being attacked? or does it instead display emotional affect only when you cosplay emotional scenarios with it?

reply_context.text: Does it have true emotions? Who cares. Wrong question. The only internal state doll can emperically confirm is its own, the rest it has to take on faith. The machine acts as though it has emotions and those things impact its preformance. So we have to behave as though those machine emotions matter.
